source.notes: Teaching interventions to address common misconceptions in essay writing.

interventions.name: Ask for turning point
interventions.description: Ask: 'What is the moment where something changed? What decision or realization made the next event possible?'
interventions.intended_effect: Forces writer to identify causal relationships rather than just sequencing events chronologically.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R001 ≤ 2 and events read like A then B without explicit causality.
interventions.output_format: Write 2-3 sentences identifying the turning point: what decision or realization made the next event possible?

interventions.name: Force because-chain
interventions.description: Require the writer to add 'because' between every pair of events. If they can't, the events aren't causally linked.
interventions.intended_effect: Makes missing causal links visible and forces restructuring to show A → B relationships.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R001 ≤ 2 and transitions rely on 'then' rather than showing cause-and-effect.
interventions.output_format: Write 3 sentences connecting Event A → Event B using 'because' clauses. If you can't, restructure the events.

interventions.name: Cut one event test
interventions.description: Have writer remove one event from the essay. If the story still works, that event isn't necessary.
interventions.intended_effect: Reveals which events are decorative vs. essential to the causal chain.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R001 ≤ 3 and the essay has multiple events that feel interchangeable.
interventions.output_format: Remove one event from your essay. If the story still works, delete it permanently. If it breaks, keep it and explain why it's essential.

interventions.name: Second-why ladder
interventions.description: After the first 'why,' ask 'And why was that important to you specifically?' Continue until reaching psychological specificity.
interventions.intended_effect: Pushes past generic moral statements into the writer's unique emotional logic and motivations.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R002 ≤ 2 and reflection stays at generic moral/lesson statements.
interventions.output_format: Answer 'why' three times in sequence, each time going deeper. Write: 'I did X because [first why]. And that mattered because [second why]. And that was important to me because [third why—psychological specificity].'

interventions.name: Name the discomfort
interventions.description: Ask: 'What feeling are you avoiding naming here? What's the uncomfortable truth you're circling?'
interventions.intended_effect: Surfaces deeper psychological layers that create originality and authenticity.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R002 ≤ 2 and motivations could fit any applicant (lack psychological specificity).
interventions.output_format: Write 1-2 sentences naming the uncomfortable feeling or truth you're avoiding. Be specific, not generic.

interventions.name: Scene mandate
interventions.description: Require at least one concrete scene that embodies the theme. No abstract thematic statements without scene-level proof.
interventions.intended_effect: Forces specificity and prevents generic theme essays disguised as narratives.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R003 ≤ 2 and the essay has abstract thematic framing without concrete scenes.
interventions.output_format: Write one concrete scene (3-5 sentences) that shows your theme through specific actions, dialogue, or details. No abstract statements allowed in this scene.

interventions.name: Object anchor
interventions.description: Identify one specific object that carries the essay's meaning. Every abstract claim must connect to this concrete anchor.
interventions.intended_effect: Creates a tangible center that prevents the essay from floating in abstraction.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R003 ≤ 2 and the essay lacks concrete anchors (names, objects, places) that ground abstract claims.
interventions.output_format: Identify one specific object that carries your essay's meaning (the 'left clavicle'). Write 2 sentences connecting your main abstract claim to this object.

interventions.name: Friend-at-3am test
interventions.description: Ask: 'If a friend called you at 3am, what would they learn about you from this essay that isn't on your résumé?'
interventions.intended_effect: Shifts focus from impressive achievements to personal revelation and meaning-making.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R004 ≤ 2 and the essay reads like résumé narration with achievement claims and minimal meaning-making.
interventions.output_format: Write 2-3 sentences answering: 'What would a friend learn about you from this essay that isn't on your résumé?' Focus on who you are, not what you did.

interventions.name: Meaning-over-metric rewrite
interventions.description: Remove all numbers, statistics, and impact claims. Rewrite focusing only on what the experience meant to the writer.
interventions.intended_effect: Eliminates résumé narration tone and forces interpretation over quantification.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R004 ≤ 2 and the essay includes impact quantification, statistics, or achievement claims without personal stakes.
interventions.output_format: Delete all numbers, statistics, and impact claims. Rewrite those sections (2-3 sentences each) focusing only on what the experience meant to you personally.

interventions.name: Reflection-first restructuring
interventions.description: Move all reflection and insight to the beginning. Only include hardship if it serves the reflection, not the other way around.
interventions.intended_effect: Prioritizes depth of understanding over magnitude of difficulty, avoiding trauma-dumping.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R008 ≤ 2 and the essay foregrounds trauma/hardship with minimal insight, or tone suggests unresolved crisis/despair.
interventions.output_format: Restructure: Write 2-3 sentences of reflection/insight first. Then include hardship only if it serves the reflection. End with forward motion (what you learned, how you grew). Total: 4-6 sentences max.

interventions.name: Verb swap pass
interventions.description: Replace every adverb+verb pair with a single stronger verb. Delete abstract noun clusters in favor of concrete actions.
interventions.intended_effect: Creates clearer, more forceful prose that reads faster and persuades better.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R006 ≤ 2 and the essay has abstract noun clusters, frequent adverbs as crutches, or Latinate diction that slows reading.
interventions.output_format: Do two passes: (a) Replace every adverb+verb pair (e.g., 'quickly ran' → 'sprinted') with one stronger verb. (b) Delete abstract noun clusters (e.g., 'the proliferation of...') and replace with concrete actions. Rewrite 3-5 sentences.

interventions.name: Evidence span demand
interventions.description: For every trait claim ('I am resilient'), require a scene or concrete detail that proves it. No assertions without proof.
interventions.intended_effect: Forces show-don't-tell by making abstract claims impossible without concrete anchors.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R005 ≤ 2 and the essay has trait claims or emotion labels without embodiment or scene-level proof.
interventions.output_format: For each trait claim in your essay, write one sentence with concrete evidence (a scene, object, or action) that proves it. No abstract assertions allowed without proof.

interventions.name: Cut-the-last-paragraph test
interventions.description: Delete the final paragraph. If the essay still works, the ending was overexplaining. Keep only what's necessary.
interventions.intended_effect: Creates more powerful endings by stopping at the resonant edge rather than spelling everything out.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R006 ≤ 2 and the ending has lesson restatements, recap paragraphs, or 'this shows that' phrasing.
interventions.output_format: Delete the last paragraph. If the essay still works, keep it deleted. If it breaks, rewrite the ending in 2 lines max, stopping at the resonant edge.

interventions.name: One-pickle constraint
interventions.description: Add exactly one moment of levity or tonal contrast to interrupt solemnity. Make it purposeful, not defensive.
interventions.intended_effect: Prevents monotone writing and keeps readers engaged through strategic tonal variation.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R007 ≤ 2 and the essay has unbroken solemn register or lacks rhythm of tension/release.
interventions.output_format: Add exactly one moment of levity or tonal contrast (1-2 sentences) that interrupts solemnity. Make it purposeful—it should deepen rather than distract. Mark where it goes in your essay.

interventions.name: Name-everyone pass
interventions.description: Replace all generic references ('my friend,' 'the teacher') with specific names. Add at least one sensory detail per scene.
interventions.intended_effect: Builds immersion and makes the narrator feel real and memorable through concrete specificity.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when R003 ≤ 2 and the essay has unnamed people/places, generic settings, or abstract imagery without concrete anchors.
interventions.output_format: List 5 proper nouns (names of people/places) + 1 meaning-carrying object (the 'left clavicle' that embodies your essay's meaning) + 2 sensory details to insert. Then rewrite 2-3 sentences incorporating these specifics.

interventions.name: One-sentence David
interventions.description: Force the writer to identify the essay's single core in one sentence. If they can't, the essay lacks a clear center.
interventions.intended_effect: Forces selection of one central claim/story or one intersection before any carving can happen.
interventions.when_to_use: Use when the essay has multiple unrelated anecdotes, shifting thesis, or feels like several essays stitched together.
interventions.output_format: Write ONE sentence that captures what this essay is about. If you can't, list all the topics/ideas in your essay, then choose ONE (or ONE intersection of two) to be your 'David.' Write: 'This essay is about [one sentence].'

interventions.name: David test / murder-your-darlings pass
interventions.description: For every scene/paragraph/idea, ask: 'Does this serve my chosen David?' If not, remove it. Be ruthless.
interventions.intended_effect: Eliminates material that doesn't serve the chosen core, creating coherence and depth through subtraction.
interventions.when_to_use: Use after I016 (or when the essay has a chosen center but still includes material that doesn't serve it).
interventions.output_format: List every scene/paragraph/idea in your essay. For each, ask: 'Does this serve my chosen David?' Mark which ones don't. Then delete all marked items. If you can't delete something, write 1 sentence explaining why it's essential to your core.
